name: BJ Fogg Behavior Model + Hooked (Eyal) — Habit Formation
definition_origin: BJ Fogg formalized the Fogg Behavior Model (B = MAT, later renamed B = MAP) at Stanford's Behavior Design Lab in the early 2000s; canonized in his 2019 book 'Tiny Habits'. Nir Eyal published 'Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products' (Penguin/Portfolio, 2014), introducing the four-phase Hook loop (Trigger → Action → Variable Reward → Investment), a synthesis of Fogg's work, BF Skinner's variable schedule reinforcement, and consumer psychology.
core_mechanism: Fogg: Behavior occurs only when Motivation (sensation/anticipation/belonging), Ability (a function of the scarcest of: time, money, physical effort, brain cycles, social deviance, non-routine) and a Prompt (Spark/Facilitator/Signal) converge — multiplicatively, so any zero kills the behavior. Eyal layers a feedback loop: an external Trigger creates an internal trigger (emotion, usually negative — boredom, loneliness, FOMO); the user takes the smallest possible Action; receives a Variable Reward (of the tribe / of the hunt / of the self), where unpredictability — per Skinner — drives the strongest dopamine release in the mesolimbic pathway; then makes an Investment (data, content, followers, streak length) that increases switching cost and pre-loads the next Trigger. Repeated cycling until the internal trigger replaces the external one is what defines a habit.
neuroscience_correlate: Mesolimbic dopamine system, specifically nucleus accumbens activation under variable-ratio reinforcement schedules (Schultz 1997, Berridge 'wanting' vs. 'liking' distinction). Anticipation of variable reward — not the reward itself — is the dopamine spike. Same circuitry as gambling addiction and compulsive social-media checking.
empirical_evidence: Duolingo is the most-cited applied case: post-streak-system, the company reports ~21% lift in current-user retention and a 4.5x DAU increase over four years, reaching 113M MAU by Q3 2024 (74M MAU in 2023). Internal Duolingo blog posts (2024) credit streak protection (Streak Freeze, Streak Repair, Friends Quest) as the highest-leverage retention feature. Fogg's own clinical Tiny Habits research (n>40,000 self-reports, multiple wellness-app trials, longitudinal but methodologically self-selected) reports >70% habit-completion when behaviors are anchored + celebrated. No large-N RCT of the full Hook loop exists; most evidence is industry case-study (Pinterest, Instagram, Snapchat) re-engineered post-hoc against the framework. Counter-evidence: Allcott et al. 2020 Facebook deactivation study (n=2,844) found mood improved and time freed when the loop was broken; 2018 APA Stress in America survey: 65% of constant checkers report it harms wellbeing.
measurable_outcome_metric: D1/D7/D30 retention curves, streak-active rate, internal-trigger ratio (DAU sessions launched without external prompt / total sessions), session frequency, habit moment count (Eyal: a habit forms when an action is performed without conscious deliberation, typically after ~6+ deliberate cycles).
engineering_tactics: Make the action absurdly small — Duolingo's 1-XP 'practice' tile, Tiny Habits' 'two pushups after toilet flush'. Trigger architecture: schedule the first push notification at the user's empirically-detected peak engagement window (Duolingo runs an ML model that learns each user's pattern and sends at the time most likely to trigger a session); use lockscreen Live Activities (iOS) and Glanceable Surfaces (Android Wear) to keep the trigger visible without an open. Variable reward implementation: never let the celebration be deterministic — randomize confetti/sparkle intensity, surprise badges, occasional 'jackpot' XP multipliers. Investment: persist user-generated state (streak count, custom avatar, friend graph, saved progress) so the cost of leaving rises. Code patterns: React Native Reanimated 3 with worklet-based confetti (60fps), `expo-notifications` with adaptive scheduling, server-side variable-reward logic to prevent client tampering. Streak-save grace period (Duolingo: Streak Freeze auto-applies once per week) softens loss-aversion punishment without breaking the loop.
real_world_examples: (1) Duolingo streak-loss notification copy 'Don't let Duo down!' paired with the canonical sad-Duo lockscreen image (2023+); the 365-day streak unlocks a permanent profile flame and a public leaderboard slot — pure Investment + tribe Variable Reward. (2) Phantom Wallet's 'spin the wheel' on first connection of a wallet — visceral, pseudo-random reward, identical mechanic to slot machines, called out in the May 2025 'Weirdly Addictive Apps' video as canonical Hook deployment in fintech. (3) Snapchat Snapstreak: fire emoji with day-count next to a friend's name; missing a day breaks the streak for both users — Eyal himself cites this as the canonical 'social investment' anti-pattern. (4) Instagram pull-to-refresh: literal slot-machine arm gesture, variable feed reward (cited by Tristan Harris / Center for Humane Technology). (5) BJ Fogg's own Tiny Habits emails: anchor-based prompts with celebration scripts ('After I [anchor], I will [tiny behavior], and I will [celebration like fist-pump]').
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile is the native habitat — push notifications, lockscreen presence, Live Activities (iOS 16+), AlwaysOn AOD (Android), haptic-driven reward feedback, and biometric-fast re-entry (Face ID / fingerprint) make Triggers cheaper and Actions easier than on web. Web habit formation is weaker and typically depends on tab-pinning, browser notifications (which most users dismiss), and email Triggers. Streaks on web (Wordle, NYT Games) survive because the habit Anchor is the morning routine, not the device.
platform_design_language_alignment: Aligns cleanly with Material 3 Expressive (motion springs, shape-morphing buttons, and celebration animations are first-class M3 Expressive primitives) and with Apple's Liquid Glass interactive variants (`.glassEffect(.interactive)` shimmer on tap is a Variable-Reward delivery surface). Both 2025 platform languages explicitly invite the celebration moment.
claude_design_application: Be explicit about the four phases as discrete components Claude must wire together. Example fragment 1: 'Build a daily-practice screen using React Native Reanimated 3. The Trigger is a scheduled local notification (use expo-notifications, schedule at user-selected hour ±15min jitter for variable timing). The Action is a single primary button labeled "Practice 1 minute" with `useSharedValue` scale spring on press (damping 12, stiffness 180). On completion, fire a Variable Reward: randomly choose from 3 tiers of confetti density via a worklet — 70% small, 25% medium, 5% jackpot with screen-flash. The Investment is a server-persisted streak counter visible at the top with a flame icon that grows by 1pt per day.' Example fragment 2: 'Implement a Streak Freeze pattern: when user misses a day, do NOT reset to zero on first miss; instead show a modal "We saved your streak — use a Freeze?" with a single tap accept. Persist freezes_remaining in user state. This softens loss aversion without breaking the loop.'
stitch_application: Stitch must be told the loop is the artifact. Example fragment 1: 'Generate a habit-tracker home screen, mobile, 390x844. Top: large flame emoji icon at 64pt with the number 47 (streak days) next to it in Cabinet Grotesk Bold 56pt — this is the Investment surface, must dominate visual hierarchy. Below: today's three tasks as full-width cards 80pt tall with rounded-2xl, each with a single primary CTA "Start (1 min)" — Action must look effortless. Bottom-right: a floating purple-to-pink-gradient FAB with subtle pulse animation — this is the Trigger callout. Below the streak: a 7-day calendar strip showing past completions as filled flame dots and today as a hollow flame dot.' Example fragment 2: 'Generate a lesson-complete celebration screen: full-screen confetti burst (mixed colors, dense at center), a green checkmark in a 120pt circle, a +XP gain animation showing "+18 XP" and an unexpected secondary reward badge "Combo Bonus +5 XP" appearing 0.4s after the primary — variable, surprising. CTA "Continue" in pill button, only enabled after 0.7s celebration delay.'
ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen typically (1) makes the celebration too predictable (same confetti every time, killing dopamine novelty — explicitly request 'three randomized celebration tiers with weighted probability'), (2) over-spaces the action button, making it 'feel' like a high-friction commitment — counter with 'minimum-effort tap target ≥48dp, single-line copy starting with verb of duration ≤2 minutes', (3) defaults to deterministic streak math that resets to zero, missing the Streak Freeze / soft-loss pattern entirely — explicit prompt 'never reset streak to 0 on first miss; implement freeze grace period'. Also defaults to Inter font and shadcn cards which read as AI-slop and dilute brand investment — explicitly forbid these for the streak component.
anti_patterns: (1) Deterministic rewards that train tolerance — same confetti every win is functionally a fixed-ratio schedule and extinguishes engagement. (2) Punitive loss aversion — Duolingo's 'I'm afraid of people who skip lessons' notification (2023 incident) generated viral backlash and reduced retention in the cohort that received it. (3) Manufactured social pressure with no off-ramp (Snapstreak with no 'pause' option), producing teen anxiety. (4) Ever-escalating investment requirements (level-up cost curves designed for whales) that tip the loop into gambling psychology. (5) Triggers that exploit negative internal triggers without ever satisfying the user-stated goal (notification 'You haven't journaled today' on a meditation app — drives shame, not behavior).
regulatory_risk: High. The EU Digital Fairness Act (Commission tabling Q3-late 2026, expected adoption late 2027 / early 2028) explicitly names 'addictive design' and dopamine loops alongside dark patterns; streaks, variable rewards, FOMO notifications, and infinite-scroll are all named patterns. UK Age Appropriate Design Code (in force) prohibits 'nudge techniques' that encourage oversharing or extended use by minors. COPPA (under-13) and GDPR Article 8 (under-16) require verifiable parental consent for behavioral profiling, which the Hook loop almost always entails. June 2025 BEUC SHEIN complaint signaled regulator appetite. Legal teams should audit any streak/notification/FOMO mechanic for minors and high-vulnerability cohorts before EU launch.
pitfalls_caveats: Habit formation is amoral — same mechanic powers Duolingo (genuine learning) and infinite-scroll TikTok (attention extraction). Eyal's own Manipulation Matrix is the recommended ethical filter: would the designer use this product themselves, and does it materially improve the user's life? If 'no' on either, the pattern is manipulation. Accessibility cost: streak loss creates anxiety for users with OCD, ADHD, and depression — provide a 'pause streak' affordance. Performance: variable-reward animations at 60-120fps can drain battery; use Reanimated worklets, not JS-thread Animated. Honest signal: a habit that survives a 7-day app uninstall is intrinsically motivated; a habit that collapses is extrinsic (Hook-induced) — measure both.
cross_cultural_validity: The underlying dopamine mechanism is species-universal, but cultural expression varies: collectivist markets (CN/JP/KR) respond more strongly to Variable Rewards 'of the tribe' (leaderboards, group quests, gifting mechanics — see WeChat red envelopes, Honor of Kings clan systems) than to individual streak counters. Western individualist framing ('YOUR streak') under-performs vs. 'OUR clan streak' in these markets. RTL markets (AR) require streak/progress UI to be mirrored. 'Don't let Duo down' guilt copy translates badly across cultures — direct shame microcopy reads aggressive in JP/DE, motivating in some LATAM markets; localize tone, not just words. [uncertain] specific effect-size data for cross-cultural Hook outcomes is largely unpublished — most evidence is anecdotal industry post-mortems.
sources: [How Duolingo Gamified Monthly Active Users — The PM Repo](https://www.thepmrepo.com/articles/how-duolingo-gamified-monthly-active-users-lessons-in-habit-formation)
[Axbom — How Nir Eyal's habit books are dangerous (counter-position)](https://axbom.com/nir-eyal-habit-danger/)
[Goodwin — From Dark Patterns to Fair Play: DFA analysis (Nov 2025)](https://www.goodwinlaw.com/en/insights/publications/2025/11/alerts-practices-antc-from-dark-patterns-to-fair-play)
